coredata.dc:title: Ag and carbon quantum dot-modified Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants and hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: This study evaluates the performance of synthesized Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as photocatalysts for enhancing hydrogen production and pollutant elimination. Silver (Ag) nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were integrated into the nanocomposite to improve photocatalytic characteristics. Structural and optical characterizations confirmed the uniform dispersion of Ag and CQDs. The results showed that Ag nanoparticles enhanced visible light absorption through surface plasmon resonance (SPR), while CQDs acted as electron mediators, facilitating effective Z-scheme charge transfer and reducing recombination rates. These modifications led to a reduction in α- Fe2O3 particle size and improved charge separation. The photocatalytic efficiencies achieved were notable, with degradation rates of 49, 74, and 99 % for methyl orange (MO), phenol, and methylene blue (MB), respectively. Additionally, the CQD-modified Fe2O3/g-C3N4 composite (QFC sample) exhibited a hydrogen evolution rate of 1005 μmol/g.h. Overall, CQDs demonstrated superior performance in promoting photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen generation, providing valuable insights for designing nanomaterial composites for environmental and energy applications.

head.ce:title: Ag and carbon quantum dot-modified Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants and hydrogen production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study evaluates the performance of synthesized Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as photocatalysts for enhancing hydrogen production and pollutant elimination. Silver (Ag) nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were integrated into the nanocomposite to improve photocatalytic characteristics. Structural and optical characterizations confirmed the uniform dispersion of Ag and CQDs. The results showed that Ag nanoparticles enhanced visible light absorption through surface plasmon resonance (SPR), while CQDs acted as electron mediators, facilitating effective Z-scheme charge transfer and reducing recombination rates. These modifications led to a reduction in α- Fe2O3 particle size and improved charge separation. The photocatalytic efficiencies achieved were notable, with degradation rates of 49, 74, and 99 % for methyl orange (MO), phenol, and methylene blue (MB), respectively. Additionally, the CQD-modified Fe2O3/g-C3N4 composite (QFC sample) exhibited a hydrogen evolution rate of 1005 μmol/g.h. Overall, CQDs demonstrated superior performance in promoting photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen generation, providing valuable insights for designing nanomaterial composites for environmental and energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to enhanced urban and societal growth, the two main problems facing the world today, environmental pollution and the need to provide energy, have been the subject of relatively more research [1]. Photocatalytic decomposition of organic pollutants and water splitting are the most effective methods to solve these problems [2,3]. Recently, hydrogen was revealed to be one of the most plausible sorts of energy [4]. International interest has been drawn toward it since it is perceived as having rich potential as a future energy source other than fossil fuels [5]. The preparation of highly efficient, appropriate band gap photocatalysts for hydrogen generation has been described in numerous efforts [6–8]. Many of the photocatalysts reviewed in the literature studies have large band gaps that allow them to evaluate hydrogen production only in UV light. This is a drawback, as UV light is just a tiny portion of the total solar power available on earth [9,10]. For an enhanced use of solar energy, nanocomposite photocatalysts capable of absorbing visible light need to be designed and synthesized [11,12].

The promising photocatalyst α-Fe2O3 is characterized by its appropriate bandgap energy of 1.8–2.2 eV, high chemical stability, low cost, and non-toxic nature [13,14]. Fe2O3 can absorb visible light, which causes the creation of electron-hole pairs that trigger redox reactions [15]. This information is expected to have numerous potential uses in various applications, such as water purification and other photocatalytic-related functions [16,17]. Nevertheless, there are some demerits of α-Fe2O3 alone, namely, low photocatalytic activity because of the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and limited charge carrier mobility. Regarding water splitting, there are disadvantages of α-Fe2O3 because it cannot adequately handle activities in this line [8]. This is attributed to the conduction band position being situated at a more positive value of 0.32 V/NHE compared to the H2 production level of H2/H2O, which is positioned at 0 V/NHE [15]. The performance of photocatalysts can be improved by compositing them with other semiconductors [18]. On the other hand, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as an organic semiconductor has received global research interest as it is thermally and chemically stable, with a favorable bandgap of 2.7 eV, and since constituent elements, carbon and nitrogen are comparatively cheap and abundant [19]. Through sp2 hybridization, π-conjugated graphitic planes form by linking tri-s-triazine ring units, but the three-coordinated bridged nitrogen atoms are excluded from the valence and conduction bands [1]. Charge transfer between tri-s-triazine ring units is challenging, as photogenerated electrons and holes are often trapped within each ring. This results in poor charge separation and low photocatalytic activity. To overcome such limitations and further boost the photocatalytic activity, several strategies have been developed, which include the incorporation of noble metals with different elements, a dye-sensitization process, control of morphology, and coupling with another semiconductor [1,8,19]. Considerable interest has been attracted by Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites in the field of photocatalysis due to their enhanced properties in charge separation and light absorption, which facilitate the effective degradation of pollutants. For example, Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite of Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 were developed using a wet impregnation technique, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance towards the degradation of DR81, a common color pollutant in textiles [20]. In another work [21], Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite was synthesized by the co-precipitation method, which improved the charge carrier separation, reduced recombination rates between the semiconductors and increased the photocatalytic activity for the degradation of the pollutant norfloxacin.

Another method to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors is by incorporating CQDs and metal nanoparticles. Numerous scientists have recently shown great interest in CQDs with an average diameter below 10 nm [22]. This is attributed to several important properties, including how well these materials dissolve in water, their ability to emit light when stimulated (up-conversion photoluminescence (PL)), and their effectiveness in transferring electrons when exposed to light. Additionally, factors like their size, electrical conductivity, stability when exposed to light, affordability, and non-toxic nature all play a significant role [23]. Combining CQDs with semiconductor photocatalysts has enhanced the photocatalytic performance [24]. In addition, the experimental results show that some plasma nanostructured metals possess high carrier mobility and absorption cross-sections. Among the metals, Ag is regarded as ideal because of its strong localized SPR effect within the 320–450 nm wavelength range [25]. This newly observed phenomenon has been termed plasma photocatalysis [26]. In this study, the fabrication of Fe2O3/CQDs nanocomposites was explored, with an emphasis on their use for the photodegradation of hazardous gases such as benzene and methanol under visible light. The results demonstrated that the photocatalytic activity of Fe2O3 was significantly enhanced by the incorporation of CQDs. This improvement was attributed to better charge separation and increased light absorption characteristics. The study highlighted the potential of these composites for environmental remediation, as they effectively degrade harmful gases present in the atmosphere [27]. Motahare Harati et al. [28] synthesized the magnetically separable Fe2O3 @ZnO/CQD nanocomposite for the photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli in the presence of light. The outcomes highlighted that the incorporation of CQDs improved the light trapping and charge carrier conductivity of the composite, leading to superior bactericidal performance compared to other photocatalysts [28]. This research focused on the prospect of microbial inactivation and water purification by CQD-modified photocatalysts. Also, in previous studies, the presence of Ag was found to enhance photocatalytic performance. For instance, a microwave-assisted method was reported [29] for the preparation of efficient Fe2O3/Ag nanocomposites. The results demonstrated the successful synthesis of the Fe2O3/Ag nanocomposite. Additionally, these bio-reduced Fe2O3/Ag nanocomposites were evaluated for their catalytic degradation capabilities toward MB, a model contaminant, and an outstanding photocatalytic degradation efficiency with a removal rate of 88.2 % was exhibited within 140 min. It is noteworthy that the charge transportation was facilitated by the Fe2O3/Ag nanoparticles, which also inhibited the recombination of electrons in the conduction band. The wet chemical reflux method was employed to synthesize α-Fe2O3 and Ag-doped α-Fe2O3 nanostructures. These nanoparticles were utilized as photocatalysts to facilitate the degradation of eosin yellow and malachite green dyes in an aqueous environment under visible light irradiation. Approximately 98 % of the dye molecules were effectively decolorized by Ag-α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles within a duration of 90 min [30].

While some studies have explored adding Ag and CQDs separately to Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 photocatalysts, the comparative impact of these two components on the photocatalytic performance of Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites remains unexamined. Therefore, the aim of this research is to systematically evaluate and compare the impact of Ag nanoparticles and CQDs on the photocatalytic activity of the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite. Unlike prior studies focusing on either hydrogen production or dye degradation, this study uniquely evaluates both photocatalytic hydrogen production from water splitting and organic dye degradation, providing a comprehensive insight into the material's versatility. Through an in-depth and detailed comparative examination of various structural, microstructural, optical, electrochemical, and photocatalytic properties, this research offers an understanding of how Ag nanoparticles and CQDs modify the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite and contribute to improved photocatalytic activity. The study aims to elucidate the mechanisms by which Ag and CQDs affect the characteristics of nanocomposite, providing valuable insights for future material design in photocatalysis. Such information is paramount for utilizing these nanocomposites for fields of photocatalysis, renewable energy, and environmental remediation.

α-Fe2O3 (Fe2O3 sample) was synthesized using a conventional precipitation method, similar to the approach used in the previous work [8]. Ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, ≥99.9 %, Merck) was utilized as the precursor. Approximately 10 g of this material was mixed with 150 mL of deionized water and stirred for 1 h. Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 25 % NH3, SIGMA-ALDRICH) was added as a precipitant until the solution's pH was raised to 13. The mixture was stirred for an additional 2 h at a temperature of 80 °C, resulting in a brown solution. Afterward, the solution was washed with water and alcohol and subsequently dried. Finally, the resulting material was calcined at 390 °C (Fig. 1 (a) ). To synthesize g-C3N4 nanosheets (g-C3N4 sample), a dry blend of C3H6N6 (≥99.9 %, Merck) and NH4Cl (≥99.9 %, Merck) in a weight ratio of one to five was first established. After the materials were completely mixed. The materials were arranged in a crucible made of alumina and sealed with a lid. The container was set inside a kiln with thermal insulation, where the heat was progressively elevated at a rate of 10 °C per minute. The temperature was sustained at 550 °C for 240 min to facilitate heat-induced polymer formation (Fig. 1 (b)) [4]. To create the nanocomposite consisting of α-Fe2O3 and 10 wt percent g-C3N4 (FC10 sample), 10 wt% g-C3N4 powder was first dispersed to the Fe2O3 precursor by a half hour ultrasonic probe session (100 W and a frequency of 50 kHz). Then, the rest of the steps were carried out like pure α-Fe2O3 synthesis. 3 wt % Ag incorporated FC10 sample (FCAg sample) was similarly synthesized. While the initial step was adjusted to include a precise amount of AgNO3 added to the solution.

CQDs were prepared using a straightforward microwave-assisted method [4]. A transparent solution was created by dissolving citric acid and urea in a 1:1 mass ratio in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was then exposed to microwave radiation at 900 W with 80 % efficiency for a duration of 5 min. After that, the resulting dark solid was mixed with 250 mL of deionized water and spun in a centrifuge at 10000 rpm to remove any larger particles. In the end, the dark solution was freeze-dried, resulting in a powder form of CQDs (Fig. 1 (c)). The method for preparing CQDs-impregnated g-C3N4 was carried out using a thermal-impregnation approach [4]. To start, a solution of CQDs was made with a concentration of 1 g/L. Then, 100 mg of g-C3N4 nanosheets were mixed into 100 mL of deionized water. To this suspension, 0.5 mL of the CQDs solution was injected, followed by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 12 h to facilitate the impregnation of CQDs onto the nanosheet surfaces. The suspension was then dried overnight at 100 °C, and the resulting powder was subjected to calcination at 300 °C for a duration of 1 h (Fig. 1(d)). This impregnated g-C3N4 with CQDs was used to synthesize 0.5 wt % CQDs incorporated α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite (QFC sample), (Fig. 1(e)), employing a preparation method similar to the preparation process of the FC10 sample.

The crystallographic features were examined using a Phillips X-ray diffractometer, identified by the plate number PW3040/60. The diffractometer utilized copper Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.54 Å. The morphology was examined using a VEGA3 TESCAN field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), while the elemental composition of the products was determined through energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). In addition, the microstructure was examined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM-FEI Tecnai G2-F20) operated at 200 kV. Using a Shimadzu MPC-2200 spectrophotometer, the band gap energy was measured through a DRS test. Additionally, a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer was utilized for PL analysis with 316 nm as the excitation wavelength. The specific surface area of the synthesized photocatalysts was measured using nitrogen adsorption at 77 K with the BELBelsorp II instrument, employing the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The photoelectrochemical characteristics of the prepared samples were assessed using a standard three-electrode cell system. A fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) surface was utilized as the working electrode for the application of photocatalyst powders. A Pt wire was chosen as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode saturated with KCl was selected as the reference electrode. A solution of 20 mL of 0.2 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at the open circuit potential, spanning a frequency range from 0.1 to 1000000 Hz, with an amplitude of 0.1 V applied. In dark conditions, a three-electrode system was utilized to create a Mott-Schottky diagram at a frequency of 1000 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. Furthermore, Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out using the same electrode under identical conditions (scan rate = 10 mV/S). The chemical bonding characteristics and elemental makeup of the samples were examined using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument that utilizes Al Kα radiation (ESCALAB MK II).

The synthesized nanomaterials' photocatalytic efficiency was evaluated by degrading an organic dye solution of MB, MO, and phenol under a 150 W OSRAM lamp with a UV filter positioned 20 cm above the solution to emit only visible light. The pollutant concentration was 2 mg/L, and a photocatalyst dosage of 1600 mg/L was utilized during the process at pH = 7. The temperature is kept at approximately 25 °C using a water-cooled system surrounding the aqueous solution. After the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was allowed to be achieved in the dark for 60 min, the solution with the photocatalyst was subjected to visible light. At 30 min intervals for 180 min, 4 mL samples were extracted and then subjected to centrifugation. The MB, MO, and phenol concentrations in the samples were analyzed using the UNICO 2100 UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer at wavelengths of 664, 464, and 270 nm, respectively. Also, a control experiment was performed where an MB solution was subjected to light in the absence of a photocatalyst. The photocatalytic mechanism and the primary reactive agents related to the degradation of MB were investigated, and trapping agents were utilized as scavengers to identify them. A concentration of 3 mM of Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), 3 mM of Isopropanol (IPA), and 0.1 mM of p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were utilized as scavengers for holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxides (•O2 ˉ), respectively.

Within a sealed gas circulation setup, hydrogen was created by irradiation of visible light from two Xenon lamps of 50 W onto a reactor that contained 1600 mg/L of photocatalyst. This process involved maintaining agitation of the 200 mL liquid solution for a total of 300 min. An MQ-8 gas sensor was used to quantify hydrogen generation. In the absence of hydrogen, the sensor presents a resistance of Ro, but when hydrogen gas is encountered, an increase in conductivity is noted, leading to a decrease in the sensor's resistance, Rs, which allows for the detection of hydrogen gas to be using the ratio of Rs to Ro for the calculation. The sensor was calibrated in this experiment using hydrogen of 99.99 % purity. In the absence of hydrogen, the proportion of Rs compared to Ro was found to be one. However, as the level of hydrogen rose, we noticed that this proportion decreased logarithmically. At a gas level of 1000 ppm, the ratio is measured at 0.09. However, this value decreases to 0.04 at 10000 ppm. Hydrogen gas production in ppm can be recorded in the device's memory by connecting the sensor ratio to a microcontroller using Arduino Uno software [8,31]. Fig. 2 illustrates the arrangement of the experimental apparatus.

The XRD results for the synthesized samples, including Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC, are depicted in Fig. 3 . The fabrication of Fe2O3 with the hexagonal structure was confirmed by the existence of nine characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 24.1 (012), 33.2 (104), 35.7 (110), 40.9 (113), 49.4 (20-4), 54.1 (116), 57.4 (108), 62.4 (214), and 64.0° (300) (Ref. No: 96-900-9783). By analyzing the location and strength of the peaks in the XRD spectrum, the sample is purely crystalline and only of the α-Fe2O3 phase with no sign of any impurity or non-crystalline phase. In the case of g-C3N4 in Fig. 3, two peaks are shown. The first one at 2θ = 12.9° belongs to the (100) plane, and the second peak at 2θ = 27.69° corresponds to the (002) plane with the higher intensity. In addition, the initial peak revealed a d-spacing value of about 0. 685 nm, equivalent to the in-plane repeat distance of tris-triazine measured to the spacing of the nitride pores within the crystalline structure. On the other hand, the second peak with the d-spacing value of 0. 321 nm is related to the slipped stacking of the conjugated double bonds in aromatics, which has a very high intensity (JCPDS No. 87–1526). To achieve the best performance based on the previous study [8], adding 10 wt% g-C3N4 to α-Fe2O3 resulted in the FC10 sample nanocomposite. Subsequently, the FC10 sample was examined using XRD, and as anticipated from the previous research, the XRD peaks indicated the correct formation of this nanocomposite. XRD analysis revealed that the incorporation of 3 wt% Ag nanoparticles and 0.5 wt% CQDs did not result in any alterations to the peaks, attributed to the low weight percentages of these components, and no new peaks were observed. The presence of Ag may not change the stability of the α-Fe2O3 phase. In the case of the sample containing CQDs, only sharper peaks were observed due to their very low amount, possibly indicating an increase in crystallites, as discussed in Table 1 . Using Eqs. (1)–(3) [8,32], the values of crystallite size, crystallinity percentage, d-spacing and lattice parameter were determined.

Where λ represents the wavelength of the incoming X-rays, θ stands for the diffraction angle, β indicates the full width at half maximum for a specific peak, Acp refers to the region beneath the crystalline peaks, and Ata signifies the overall area. Also, the sides of the crystalline structure are labeled as a, b, and c, and h, k, and l are the Miller indices. According to Eq. (1) and Table 1, the average crystallite size for the Fe2O3, FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples was analyzed to be 23, 46, 25, and 38 nm, respectively. Crystallite growth in the nanocomposite is also affected by the diffusion of Ag and CQD nanoparticles. However, the data reflect that though CQDs could marginally affect the crystallite size, the incorporation of Ag reduces the crystallite size. It seems that the presence of these nanoparticles can change the nucleation and growth kinetics during the formation of the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 composite. These observations concur with findings that Ag nanoparticles can prevent the growth of large crystallites by stabilizing the small grain sizes [33,34]. The introduction of Ag nanoparticles can also induce lattice strain in the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 matrix. This strain can create defects in the crystal structure, which can serve as active sites for photocatalytic reactions. Defects can also facilitate charge carrier separation by providing pathways for electron transfer, thereby reducing recombination rates.

The findings from the XRD analysis indicated the need for further investigation of the synthesized samples. FESEM analysis was employed to examine the morphology of Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples, as shown in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4 (a), spherical particles of Fe2O3 are depicted, demonstrating the effective dispersion of the synthesized particles. The average size of these particles was measured to be approximately 48 nm. In Fig. 4(b)–g-C3N4 nanosheets can be seen, which are well displayed in a layered and step-like manner. Additionally, the average thickness of the layers is about 28 nm. The FESEM image of FC10 is presented in Fig. 4 (c). Fe2O3 nanoparticles are evenly dispersed on the g-C3N4 nanosheets, and the size of the Fe2O3 particles is about 42 nm. The impact of adding Ag and CQDs on the morphology is illustrated in Fig. 4(d) and (e), showing minimal differences. The scattered presence of Fe2O3 particles on the g-C3N4 nanosheets is still seen. However, the only significant difference in these images is the reduction in the size of the Fe2O3 particles; for samples containing Ag and CQDs, the average size of Fe2O3 nanoparticles was measured as 30 nm and 28 nm, respectively. Also, the dynamics of particle growth might change because of the interaction between Ag and CQDs, which can lead to a general reduction in particle size [34]. Ag and CQDs function as nucleation sites and spacers on g-C3N4, resulting in smaller, uniformly distributed Fe2O3 particles on the surface of g-C3N4. This optimizes interfacial area, reduces carrier pathways, and increases light absorption collectively improving charge separation and photocatalytic efficiency. Also, CQDs can act as stabilizing agents that prevent the agglomeration of Fe2O3 particles during synthesis. By providing steric hindrance and electrostatic repulsion, CQDs can help maintain a smaller particle size and a more uniform dispersion of Fe2O3 on the g-C3N4 surface [4,35]. A well-dispersed system with smaller particle sizes can enhance light absorption and improve charge separation, as the nanoparticles can more effectively interact with light and the g-C3N4 matrix. Fig. 4 (f) represents the elemental mapping and weight percentage composition of the FCAg nanocomposite. The elemental maps show a uniform distribution of Fe, O, N, C, and Ag compounds. The composition analysis indicates that Fe is the dominant element at 58 wt%, followed by O at 31.1 wt%, C at 7.2 wt%, Ag at 2.9 wt%, and N at 0.8 wt%.

Since the FESEM results did not allow for a clear evaluation of the microstructural differences between the FC10 and QFC samples, TEM and HRTEM analyses were conducted on these two samples to gain a detailed understanding of their structural and morphological characteristics. The corresponding results are given in Fig. 5 . The TEM and HRTEM images of FCAg nanocomposite in Fig. 5 (a) indicate the g-C3N4 nanosheets with α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles dispersed on them, along with the presence of Ag nanoparticles. The crystalline nature of α-Fe2O3 and Ag is demonstrated by the distinct diffraction rings of each, as depicted in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in the lower left corner. (104), (110), and (113) planes belong to α-Fe2O3, while (111) and (200) planes are attributed to Ag. The SAED pattern image on the right side of Fig. 5 (a) shows distinct diffraction rings corresponding to the g-C3N4 (002) plane, further verifying the crystalline nature of the g-C3N4 element. The HRTEM image on the right side of Fig. 5 (a) also reveals the lattice fringes of Ag (200) and Fe2O3 (104) planes with a d-spacing of 0.20 nm and 0.28 nm, respectively. The interface between Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, marked by the yellow dashed line, demonstrates the successful integration of the components. Fig. 5 (b) shows the TEM image of the QFC nanocomposite. A fairly consistent arrangement of Fe2O3 nanoparticles, averaging 22 nm in size, is detected on g-C3N4 nanoplates. Valuable insights into the structural characteristics of the nanocomposites are provided by the detailed analysis of these HRTEM images. The lattice fringes of CQDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, as well as the Fe2O3 (104) plane with a spacing of 0.28 nm, are shown in the magnified regions on the bottom right side of the HRTEM image. The crystalline nature of the CQDs is further confirmed by the distinct (101) diffraction spot observed in the top right corner, which corresponds to the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern.

Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the BET surface area, pore size, and pore volume analyses of the α-Fe2O3, FC10, FCAg, and QFC nanocomposites alongside their respective quantitative data. As seen in Fig. 6 (a) the adsorption-desorption isotherms exhibit characteristics of mesoporous materials, with an increase in the adsorbed volume for nanocomposites containing g-C3N4, Ag, and CQDs compared to pure α-Fe2O3. The specific surface area (SBET) values increase from 30.1 m2/g for α-Fe2O3 to 51.7 m2/g for FC10 and further to 56.2 m2/g for FCAg. The QFC composite shows a slightly lower SBET value of 48.6 m2/g compared to FCAg, which might be attributed to the structural modifications caused by the incorporation of CQDs, due to partial blocking of the mesopores or aggregation effects. The CQDs may be more present in the smaller mesopores or the entrance of large pores, thereby decreasing the surface area available for nitrogen adsorption and at the same time increasing pore connectivity and volume [36]. This is consistent with the observed increase in total pore volume (0.080 cm3/g for QFC compared to 0.056 cm3/g for FCAg), which suggests that CQDs enhance the structural framework, albeit at a minor trade-off in specific surface area. The significant enhancement in textural properties due to nanocomposite formation is highlighted by the analysis of pore size and total pore volume, shown in Fig. 6 (b) and the inset table. While α-Fe2O3 exhibits the largest average pore size (13.38 nm), the FC10 and FCAg reduce the average pore size to 7.62 nm and 4.31 nm, respectively, suggesting that these modifications promote finer mesoporosity. Interestingly, the QFC composite exhibits a moderately larger pore size (6.08 nm) compared to FCAg, alongside the highest total pore volume (0.080 cm3/g), indicating improved pore accessibility due to the presence of CQDs. The superior performance of the FCAg and QFC composites suggests that both Ag and CQDs significantly influence the structural properties of the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 matrix.

Further surface morphology and chemical forms of the FCAg, as well as bonding interactions between the two components, were determined by using XPS analysis. The survey spectrum indicates the synthesis of the FCAg nanocomposite by peaks at approximately 710, 398, 531, 286, and 368 eV for Fe 2p, N1s, O1s, C1s, and Ag 3d, respectively. From Fe 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum in Fig. 7 (b) , four peaks are found at 709.9, 710.58, 710.79, and 724.68 eV, corresponding to Fe3+, Fe2+, Fe 3p 2/3, and Fe 2p 1/2, respectively [19]. The peaks related to O 1s are depicted in Fig. 7 (c). This spectrum reveals two peaks. The initial peak, found at 529.52 eV, is associated with the binding energy linked to the interaction between Fe–O–C. The subsequent peak, observed at 532.11 eV, pertains to the Fe–O bond of Fe2O3 within the sample [8]. In the C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 7 (d), two prominent peaks at 284.93 and 286.84 eV are present, primarily arising from the g-C3N4 phase. These peaks can be attributed to C–C and N–C=N bonding [37]. There are two peaks at 398.63 and 398.88 eV in the N1s spectrum belonging to the N–C3, C–N=C functional groups in Fig. 7 (e) [38]. The high-resolution Ag 3d peaks shown in Fig. 7 (f) reveal two main peaks at 368.3 and 373.9 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively [39].

An extensive study of the optical properties can provide a comprehensive evaluation of the photocatalytic efficiency and investigate the effect of Ag and CQDs in the modified nanocomposite. DRS and PL analyses were used to examine these properties and achieve a correct comprehension of the optical properties. In Fig. 8 (a) , the absorption limits of pure g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3 are observed at approximately 470 and 520 nm, respectively. The FC10 composite exhibits intermediate behavior, combining the absorption characteristics of α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, thus broadening the light absorption into both visible and UV regions. The inclusion of Ag further enhances light absorption through the localized SPR effects of silver nanoparticles [40]. The nanocomposite samples, in general, demonstrate a significant capacity for light absorption in the visible range. This can be explained by the reduction in particle size following the formation of the nanocomposite from these materials [41]. Moreover, both Ag and CQDs can serve as electron traps or facilitate charge transfer, offering new pathways for electron excitation and recombination. These interactions can alter the practical energy levels, which in turn shift the absorption spectra [42,43]. The reduction in the band gap is attributed to changes in electronic bonding, as previously discussed [44]. In this regard, the bandgap energies are determined by Tauc equation (4) [45].

)
The symbols representing the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, vibrational frequency, a constant, and band gap energy are h, v, A, and Eg, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 (b), the band gap energies for pure α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 were determined to be 1.9 and 2.76 eV, respectively. Similar results have been found in previous studies [8,19]. The band gaps of the FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples were approximately 2.31, 2.24, and 2.17 eV, respectively. The improvement of light absorption characteristics of the nanocomposites is attributed to the incorporation of metal nanoparticles like Ag, which is seen as an advantage [46]. This characteristic thus serves a significant function in improving the photocatalytic performance of the material, which is why a range of solar wavelengths is effectively harnessed for the catalysis process. In addition, when CQDs are incorporated into the FC10 matrix, a progressive reduction of the overall band gap energy of the compound is observed. This alteration improves the ability to trap or harvest visible light, which is believed to be important for photocatalytic processes [47].

PL spectroscopy was employed to investigate electron-hole conversions and recombination processes [48,49], as shown in Fig. 8 (c). The FC10 sample indicates that the PL intensity is significantly reduced compared to the pure samples. The addition of g-C3N4 can reduce the PL emission of α-Fe2O3 by acting as an electron sink [50]. As photogenerated electrons transfer from α-Fe2O3 to g-C3N4, fewer electrons remain in α-Fe2O3 to recombine with holes, which reduces the radiative recombination. The suppression of electron-hole recombination is facilitated by both Ag nanoparticles and CQDs through the enhancement of charge separation and the provision of additional pathways for electron transfer [51,52]. As a result, lower radiative recombination is achieved, leading to a decrease in PL intensity in the nanocomposite compared to FC10 alone.

The results of the EIS analysis for the synthesized samples can be seen in the Nyquist diagram in Fig. 9 (a) . This result is illustrated using an equivalent circuit, where Rs is the ohmic resistance, CPE is the constant phase element, RCT is the charge transfer resistance, and ZW is the Warburg impedance. The obtained Rct values demonstrate significant differences in the charge transfer properties of the samples, with QFC showing the smallest Rct (2.856 Ω), indicating exceptional charge separation and transfer efficiency. According to Fig. 9 (a), the smallest semicircle is shown by the QFC sample, indicating the lowest charge transfer resistance compared to the other samples. It is demonstrated that the addition of CQD to the α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite significantly increases the charge separation efficiency, leading to a reduction in recombination and an increase in photocatalytic activity. It is commonly noted that a smaller arc is associated with reduced charge transfer resistance, resulting in more effective separation and transfer of electrons and holes, which enhances photocatalytic activity [53]. In contrast, the other samples exhibit much higher Rct values, with g-C3N4 showing the largest Rct (423.28 kΩ), reflecting poor charge transfer efficiency and limited photocatalytic performance. α-Fe2O3 and FC10 also display relatively high Rct values (63.139 kΩ and 91.348 kΩ, respectively), suggesting that these materials, while slightly improved in charge transfer efficiency, do not match the performance of QFC or FCAg. FCAg, with an Rct of 49.762 kΩ, demonstrates improved charge separation compared to the other samples (except QFC), attributed to the addition of Ag nanoparticles, which enhance conductivity through the plasmonic effect. This pronounced difference in Rct highlights that CQDs in the QFC sample, by acting as highly efficient electron mediators and promoting Z-scheme charge transfer, drastically lower the resistance to interfacial charge transfer, while Ag nanoparticles in the FCAg sample contribute to a more moderate Rct reduction by enhancing electron conductivity and facilitating charge separation, partly through their plasmonic effect. The inclusion of the Warburg impedance in QFC's EIS fitting further demonstrates its enhanced ability to support ionic diffusion and interfacial charge transfer, emphasizing its superior performance in photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical applications.

The results of the photocurrent analysis are shown in Fig. 9 (b). For added clarity, the α-Fe2O3, g-C3N4, and FC10 samples are displayed as insets in Fig. 9 (b). Among all the samples, the QFC sample exhibited the greatest photocurrent, about three times larger than that of the pure α-Fe2O3 sample. This result is also in line with the outcomes of the PL and EIS analyses that suggest that the QFC sample is optimal regarding the charge carrier's characteristics. To examine the optical properties of nanocomposites in more detail, for example, in nanocomposite FCAg, due to the presence of Ag, this nanocomposite showed better performance. Due to its plasmonic property, Ag increases the local electromagnetic field around the nanocomposite, which leads to a higher photocurrent [54]. It should be mentioned that CQD effectively deactivates surface defects in semiconductors, which is very important to reduce recombination paths [55,56]. This improves the quantum efficiency of the sample, facilitates charge generation and transfer, and ultimately improves photocurrent response.

The LSV test is represented in Fig. 9 (c), where current density is plotted with respect to applied potential, which shows the effectiveness of the generation of charges and their transfer. It can be concluded from the data in this graph that a significant increase in current density is exhibited by the QFC sample compared to the other samples. The maximum optical density is recorded at approximately −30 mA/cm2 at a potential of about 0.02 V (vs. Calomel). This value is notably higher than the current densities observed for pure α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4. This observation indicates that the nanocomposite exhibits superior carrier production and transfer efficiency. The sharp increase in current density for QFC at low potentials further supports the conclusion that the incorporation of CQDs enhances the overall photoelectrochemical properties of the system by facilitating more efficient electron transfer and reducing recombination [57]. The lowest performance is observed in samples containing α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, which agree with the conclusions made according to EIS results. The increased efficiency of the FC10 and FCAg samples, in comparison to pure α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, can be attributed to the synergistic properties. The combination of g-C3N4 with α-Fe2O3 is noted, along with the additional enhancement provided by Ag nanoparticles in the FCAg sample.

In this research, samples were tested for their photocatalytic performance using MB, MO, and phenol pollutants at a concentration of 2 mg/L, with a dosage of 1600 mg/L. The results related to MB degradation are given in Figs. 10 and 11
. The absorption spectra of MB degradation by various samples, including a blank, Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC, are presented in Fig. 10. The blank sample (in Fig. 10 (a)) shows a consistent peak around 664 nm, with minimal variation at different time intervals, indicating no significant change in absorbance. In contrast, the Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples show a decrease in absorbance as degradation time increases (Fig. 10 (b, c, d, e, and f)). The C to C0 ratio is calculated and further discussed in Fig. 11 based on the maximum peak.

The concentration of MB showed no significant variation throughout the blank test, indicating that no notable photolysis of the solution occurred based on the results of the blank test in Fig. 11 (a). The Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 samples exhibit degradation efficiencies of 40 and 51 %, respectively, after 3 h. The photodegradation of MB in the same period of time is greatly improved and reaches 88 % when using a combination of α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 in the FC10 sample. However, with the presence of Ag and CQDs in FCAg and QFC nanocomposites, the complete degradation of MB occurred after 3 h under 150 W with light irradiation. The absorption capacity and degradation rate of each sample were calculated to understand the role of the pollutant surface adsorption process and degradation kinetics separately. The reaction kinetics of the MB degradation was studied using a pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (5)) [58].

The rate constant of the pseudo-first-order model is denoted as k (1/min), the initial concentration is represented as C0 (mg/L), time is denoted as t, and the concentration at time t is denoted as C. Fig. 11 (b) shows the linear correlation of ln (C0/C) versus the time. It indicates a well-fitted with R2 values higher than 0.99 for degradation using a pseudo-first-order kinetics model. The corresponding k values, derived from the slope of these linear plots, are displayed in Fig. 11 (c). The k values for α-Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples were determined to be 0.0027, 0.0037, 0.0101, 0.0198, and 0.0247 1/min, respectively. The adsorption capacity was assessed using Eq. C0 denotes the initial concentration (mg/L), while Ce represents the concentration at equilibrium, measured in mg/L. V refers to the solution volume, and m indicates the mass of the photocatalyst. Fig. 11 (c) shows the values of Qe and k for different synthesized samples. The Qe values for α-Fe2O3, g-C3N4, FC10, FCAg, and QFC samples were found to be 0.250, 0.311, 0.323, 0.388, and 0.235 mg/g, respectively. The results indicate the enhanced photocatalytic activity for FCAg and QFC samples. FCAg Sample exhibited higher absorption, while a higher degradation rate was observed in the QFC sample. This is possibly related to several reasons. First, the property of CQDs and Ag particles, being small in size and having a large surface area, makes them impose nucleation sites during the formation of nanoparticles, which are critical for improved photocatalytic activity [34,35,59]. Secondly, the enhanced photocatalytic performance might be obtained by compounding CQDs with α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 synergistically. CQDs can serve as electron reservoirs that stabilize electrons generated by α-Fe2O3 under light irradiation [60]. This stabilization avoids recombination and allows longer contact of these electrons with MB molecules, enhancing their degradation. Ag nanoparticles exhibit strong SPR, which allows them to absorb visible light effectively. When illuminated, these nanoparticles can generate localized electric fields that enhance the light absorption of the surrounding semiconductor material (Fe2O3/g-C3N4). This increased light absorption leads to a higher generation of electron-hole pairs, which are crucial for photocatalytic reactions [61]. Also, the energy levels of Ag nanoparticles can facilitate charge transfer between the nanoparticles and the semiconductor matrix. When Ag nanoparticles are incorporated into the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 system, they can create a favorable energy band alignment that promotes the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the semiconductor to the Ag nanoparticles. This transfer helps to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the overall photocatalytic efficiency. As mentioned, FCAg light absorption was better than QFC in the dark. This can likely be attributed to the SPR effect because Ag nanoparticles show higher adsorption intensity than other non-plasmonic materials [62]. It is important to mention that despite the lower adsorption capacity of the QFC sample, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics over this sample is about 25 % faster. For this reason, in the end, it had more degradation efficiency and reached 99 %. CQDs possess unique optical properties, including size-tunable photoluminescence and a high surface area. CQDs can act as electron donors or acceptors, depending on the energy levels involved. When integrated into the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite, CQDs can capture photogenerated electrons, further improving charge separation and extending the lifetime of the charge carriers [63]. Both Ag nanoparticles and CQDs can broaden the absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite. CQDs, in particular, can absorb light in the visible range and can also act as a sensitizer, transferring energy to the semiconductor. This broadening of the absorption spectrum allows for more efficient utilization of the solar spectrum, leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity [64,65]. Totally, the evaluation of the degradation mechanism, degradation of other pollutants, stability, and recycling tests has been carried out on the QFC sample.

As shown in Fig. 12 (a) , scavenging experiments were conducted to explore the photocatalytic mechanism and the MB degradation through the addition of IPA, p-BQ, and EDTA, which are associated with hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide (•O2), and holes (h+), respectively. The active species involved were identified. According to the findings presented in Fig. 12, superoxide played the most significant role because, in the presence of p-BQ, the degradation dropped from 99 to 48 %, which indicates the active presence of superoxide in this system. Following superoxide, hydroxyl also showed strong performance. It showed a 52 % degradation in the presence of IPA, which indicates that hydroxyls also play an important role in this process. Also, holes play the least effect, with a decrease of only 14 % in the presence of EDTA. It can be concluded that the impact of the active species is as follows: •O− 2 > •OH > h+. To interpret these results, it is essential to identify the heterostructure within the nanocomposite. This requires accurately determining the positions of the conduction and valence bands. To achieve this, the Mott-Schottky test was conducted. The Mott-Schottky plots for α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 were determined using Eq. (7) [66].

In the given equation, the capacitance of the semiconductor-electrolyte interface is represented by C. The elementary charge, approximately 1.602 × 10−19 C, is denoted by e. The dielectric constant of the semiconductor is represented by ε, while the permittivity of free space, approximately 8.854 × 10−12 F/m, is indicated by ε 0. The donor density in the semiconductor is denoted by Nd, and the applied potential is represented as E. The flat band potential, defined as the potential at which the energy bands of the semiconductor are flat, is denoted by EFB. The Boltzmann constant, approximately 1.381 × 10−23 J/K, is represented by Kb, and the temperature is indicated by T. The elementary charge is also denoted by e, approximately 1.602 × 10−19 C. Fig. 12 (b) shows the curves of capacitance (1/C2) plotted against the potential for the α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 photocatalysts. A positive slope in the Mott-Schottky plot is generally interpreted as indicating the presence of an n-type semiconductor, classifying both examples as n-type [67]. The photocatalysts' flat energy levels were assessed by calculating the X-intercept. The straight section of the Mott-Schottky graph was used for the extrapolation [68]. The flat band potentials of g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3 were assessed with respect to the Calomel electrode, revealing that g-C3N4 showed a potential of −1.26 eV, whereas α-Fe2O3 exhibited a potential of 0.52 eV. These values can potentially be converted to a reference point using the NHE [69], resulting in equivalent values of −1.04 eV for g-C3N4 and 0.74 eV for α-Fe2O3. The potential of the conduction band is an essential value established from the flat band potential. In n-type semiconductors, it is commonly noted that the minimum of the CB is situated at a possible value that is roughly 0.1 V less than the associated flat band potential. For g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3, conduction band potentials of −1.14 and + 0.64 eV, respectively, are determined through calculations. Considering the importance of controlling the movement of charge carriers that are generated by light in semiconductors, the edge potential of the band for α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 samples was performed using Eqs. (8) and (9) [8].

The semiconductor material is characterized by the symbols X for electronegativity, Eg for band gap, ECB for conduction band potential, and EVB for valence band potential. Furthermore, the free energy of electrons, Eef, is measured against the hydrogen scale and is approximately 4.25 eV. A proposed charge transfer mechanism is suggested in Fig. 12 (c) to explain photocatalytic reactions based on the outcomes of radical scavenging and Mott-Schottky analyses. After light irradiation, both α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 semiconductors absorb photons with energy higher than the corresponding band gap. This leads to the excitation of electrons, which are excited from the VB to the CB (Eq. (10)). For α-Fe2O3, electrons are excited from VB at +2.56 eV to CB at +0.64 eV, while in g-C3N4 they move from VB at +1.60 eV to CB at −1.14 eV. Additionally, CQDs introduce up-conversion photoluminescence, converting lower-energy photons into higher-energy ones that can be reabsorbed by both semiconductors, thereby extending light harvesting [70]. Following photoexcitation, the transfer mechanism of the Z-scheme is enabled by CQDs. In this phase, excited electrons from the conduction band of g-C3N4 are conveyed to the CB of α-Fe2O3 through CQDs, which serve as electron transport mediators (Eq. (11)). The recombination of electron-hole pairs is inhibited by CQDs, which effectively channel excited electrons from g-C3N4 to α-Fe2O3, thereby resulting in enhanced charge separation. Mott Schottky plots confirm flat-band alignments ideal for Z-scheme transfer. EIS Nyquist data show a 40 % decrease in interfacial resistance upon CQD incorporation, evidence of faster charge transport and reduced recombination. Moreover, the intimate α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 heterojunction created in this nanocomposite furnishes a large interfacial contact area that greatly shortens the migration path of charge carriers and further suppresses bulk recombination [71]. Thus, the holes in VB of α-Fe2O3 possess a high oxidation capability, while the electrons in CB of g-C3N4 possess a strong reduction capability. Further, the charge carriers are involved in redox reactions that result in the degradation of pollutants. The process by which •O2 − is produced from the adsorbed O2 is through the reduction by electrons in the conduction band of g-C3N4 (Eq. (12)). In the Z scheme mechanism, as stated in the scavengers' tests, the production of superoxide is of the highest importance (Eq. (13)). These radicals are very reactive and can attack and break down polluting molecules. On the other hand, the holes in VB of α-Fe2O3 have a stronger oxidative potential and can oxidize water or hydroxide ions absorbed on the surface to produce hydroxyl (Eqs. (14) and (15)). These produced hydroxyl radicals are very reactive (Eq. (16)). Overall, the integration of α-Fe2O3, g-C3N4, and CQDs promotes effective charge separation, production of active superoxide and hydroxyl species, and complete decomposition of pollutants into harmless products. The dominance of •O2ˉ stems from the g-C3N4 CB potential (−1.14 eV) being far more negative than the O2/•O2ˉ potential (−0.33 eV), which drives rapid superoxide formation. By contrast, •OH production at the α-Fe2O3 VB (+2.56 eV) requires a higher-energy, one-electron oxidation of H2O/OH−, making it slower and less abundant. Thus, electrons preferentially reduce O2 to •O2ˉ (primary oxidant), while holes generate •OH only secondarily, consistent with our Z-scheme mechanism.

Fig. 13 illustrates the impact of the QFC nanocomposite on the degradation of various contaminants: MB, MO, and Phenol. Assessing and comparing the effectiveness of the synthesized photocatalyst in degrading various pollutants is highly valuable from a practical standpoint, with the findings illustrated in Fig. 13. There was no noticeable change in the blank tests even after 180 min of exposure to light. This suggests that the dye removal does not occur solely due to photolysis or the influence of light. Fig. 13 (a) illustrates that after 180 min under a light state, the MB, phenol, and MO degradation rates are 99, 74, and 49 %, respectively. To obtain more information about this issue, the calculations of Qe and k are shown in Fig. 13 (b). The maximum k value of 0.0247 1/min and the highest Qe value of 0.235 mg/g were achieved for the MB degradation. The minimum values for these two parameters are noted in the degradation of MO, with a Qe of 0.125 mg/g and a k value of 0.0032 1/min. For phenol, the Qe and k values are 0.175 mg/g and 0.0068 1/min, respectively. To explore the reasons behind the variation in degradation among the pollutants, it is essential to consider their structure and inherent characteristics. The differences in degradation efficiencies can primarily be attributed to chemical structure, adsorption characteristics, photocatalytic mechanisms, light absorption properties, and degradation pathways [72]. Each dye has a unique chemical structure, which influences its reactivity and interaction with the photocatalyst. For example, MO is an azo dye with a sulfonate group, while MB is a thiazine dye with a different structure. The presence of functional groups, molecular size, and overall polarity can affect how easily the dye molecules adsorb onto the photocatalyst surface and how they undergo degradation. The efficiency of photocatalytic degradation is often dependent on the adsorption of the dye onto the photocatalyst surface. Different dyes may have varying affinities for the photocatalyst, influenced by surface charge, hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, and steric hindrance [73]. During photo-sensitization, the electrons from MB rapidly transfer to the conduction band of the photocatalyst, leading to an enhanced rate of photodegradation. Also, as a cationic dye, MB allows for strong interactions with the negatively charged surface of g-C3N4, promoting effective adsorption and subsequent degradation by hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) generated during photocatalysis [74]. This is supported by studies indicating that hydroxyl radicals significantly contribute to the degradation process. Conversely, since MO is an anionic dye, its weaker adsorption on negatively charged surfaces can hinder its degradation efficiency [75]. This results in a significantly lower degradation rate compared to MB. The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation can vary between different dyes. For instance, some dyes may undergo direct oxidation by hydroxyl radicals (•OH), while others may be more susceptible to electron transfer processes. Also, the absorption spectrum of each dye can play a significant role. If a dye absorbs light in a wavelength range not effectively utilized by the photocatalyst, its degradation will be less efficient. Furthermore, the nature of the degradation products formed during the photocatalytic process can also influence the overall performance. Some dyes may produce more stable intermediates that are harder to degrade further, while others may break down into simpler, more easily degradable compounds [76].

The reusability of the photocatalyst material is a significant characteristic in the degradation of dye. The stability of the QFC sample, recognized as the most effective photocatalyst for degrading MB dye, was examined. Fig. 14 (a) illustrates the outcomes of the four degradation cycles. The MB degradation shows minimal reduction throughout the test, decreasing only slightly from 99 to 98 % after four cycles, indicating a very small change. The slight decrease in photocatalytic efficiency during the recycling test might be associated with the durability of the photocatalyst and the reduction in powder weight after each recycling iteration [19]. The XRD pattern of the QFC sample was examined after the fourth cycle to observe whether any changes had occurred in its structure during photodegradation, and it was found that its structure had not changed. The FESEM of the photocatalyst after the fourth cycle also indicated that there was no change in morphology, suggesting that the QFC nanocomposite has acceptable stability.

An additional key characteristic of photocatalysts is their capacity to generate hydrogen (H2) through photocatalytic water splitting. The rate of H2 production via photocatalysis under visible light was determined in various samples while using Na2SO3 as a sacrificial reagent. The results are illustrated in Fig. 15 . The blank experiment confirmed that hydrogen cannot be produced by light in water without the presence of a photocatalyst. The hydrogen production rates of 475 and 1609 μmol/g after 300 min were observed for the individual samples of α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, respectively. Following the synthesis of nanocomposites, three samples were tested for hydrogen production, and the results showed that FC10, FCAg, and QFC produced 2605, 3280, and 5027 μmol/g, respectively. To determine the kinetic constant of hydrogen production, a zero-order equation (Eq. (17)) was used [77]:
(17)

H 2
In the above formula, the amount of hydrogen produced is represented by (CH2), the time function is defined by t, and the rate constant of hydrogen production is represented by kH2. The kH2 for different samples is shown in Fig. 15 (b) and the hydrogen production rates for pure α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 samples were found to be 95 and 320 μmol/g.h, respectively. These figures are presented to have a reasonable correlation with previous work [8]. Based on the BET results and the more specific surface area of g-C3N4 compared to α-Fe2O3, it is recognized that the structural properties of g-C3N4 significantly contribute to its photocatalytic efficiency. A layered structure is exhibited by g-C3N4, which provides a high surface area and numerous active sites for reactions, thus contributing to hydrogen production [78]. Additionally, it is noted that α-Fe2O3 has a lower CB edge potential than the potential required for hydrogen production [31]. The kH2 values of the nanocomposite specifically FC10, FCAg, and QFC were measured, yielding results of 521, 656, and 1005 μmol/g·h, respectively. The positive effect of nanocomposites on effective hydrogen production was demonstrated. The higher hydrogen production rate in the FC10 nanocomposite compared to pure Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 is likely due to the creation of a heterostructure that improves light absorption in a wider range. Specifically, the reduced recombination of electron-hole pairs was linked to the optical bandgap characteristics of this nanocomposite. This outcome resulted in a large surface area, promoting efficient electron transport via the Z-scheme mechanism and ultimately enhancing the rate of hydrogen production. The increase in hydrogen production rate in the sample containing Ag can be deduced as follows. The effective transfer of electrons from the α-Fe2O3 conduction band to the g-C3N4 valence band can be achieved in a system like the one currently available, thereby minimizing electron-hole recombination, as proven by PL tests. It has been demonstrated in earlier studies [8,79] that the addition of Ag to the nanocomposite facilitates the electron transfer process and leads to an increase in hydrogen gas production within the photocatalytic system. Also, the unique surface plasmon characteristics of Ag nanoparticles result in increased light absorption in the visible spectrum, which leads to a higher availability of photons for exciting electrons and producing hydrogen [80]. Moreover, additional active sites for the reaction are provided by the Ag nanoparticles, thereby further enhancing photocatalytic activity [81]. Also, CQDs act as electron mediators, capturing the generated electrons and reducing the recombination rate of these carriers. [82]. Furthermore, it is assumed that the particle size and surface area activity of CQDs are factors influencing hydrogen generation in this system [83]. It is found that a larger surface area for charge transfer and absorption is provided by smaller CQDs, improving the photocatalytic processes. Another factor that can improve the photocatalytic activity of these nanocomposites is the synergistic effect [84]. The integration of CQDs with α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite can cause this. For example, CQDs can help stabilize charge carriers and improve their mobility in the nanocomposite structure. According to the above results and due to the higher production rate of hydrogen by QFC, this sample was considered the optimal sample. To evaluate the stability of the QFC nanocomposite for H2 production under visible light, a three-cycle recycling test was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 15 (c), it was observed that the H2 production values recorded after the second and third cycles were 4924 and 4697 μmol/g, respectively. The hydrogen production rate has been observed to drop by about 7 % after three cycles, which seems to be a reasonable number. It has been observed that the QFC sample demonstrates remarkable durability when exposed to visible light irradiation. Thereby establishes this nanocomposite as a highly effective candidate for applications in solar energy conversion.

The XPS high-resolution spectra of the QFC photocatalyst, obtained before and following H2 evolution test in Fig. 16 , exhibit perfect overlap across all core levels, indicating its chemical stability under reaction conditions. In the C 1s area (Fig. 16 (a)), the sp2 C–C peak at 284.8 eV and the N–C=N peak at 286.2 eV exhibit consistent position and intensity [37]. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 16 (b)) likewise retains the Fe3+ 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 peaks at 710.8 eV and 724.3 eV, respectively, with no new satellite features or shifts [19]. In the N 1s envelope (Fig. 16 (c)), the characteristic triazine‐N peak at 398.6 eV and the peak at 400.1 eV are unchanged [38], and the O 1s signal (Fig. 16 (d)) continues to deconvolute into lattice‐oxygen at 529.6 eV and surface‐OH/adsorbed‐H2O at 531.8 eV with identical relative areas [85]. The lack of binding-energy shifts, new peaks, or modified peak ratios in the C 1s, Fe 2p, N 1s, and O 1s regions verifies that the QFC nanocomposite preserves its structural and compositional integrity during photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Table 2 compares the photocatalytic activity of the QFC sample with previous studies. This study successfully conducted a simultaneous assessment of many contaminants, including MB, MO, and phenol, alongside hydrogen production. This indicates that the QFC nanocomposite exhibits superior performance compared to those documented in the literature.

This research effectively synthesized α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites augmented with Ag nanoparticles and CQDs, showcasing their efficacy as visible-light-driven photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution and organic pollutant degradation. Structural and optical characterizations verified that the addition of Ag and CQDs successfully decrease the particle size of Fe2O3, enhanced dispersion, and augmented interfacial contact with the g-C3N4 matrix. CQDs significantly facilitated quick charge separation and transfer, serving as electron mediators that encouraged a Z-scheme mechanism, whereas Ag nanoparticles predominantly boosted visible-light absorption via plasmonic effects. Electrochemical and photocurrent assessments validated these synergistic effects, revealing that the QFC nanocomposite exhibited the minimal charge transfer resistance and maximal photocurrent response. Evaluations of photocatalytic performance indicated that whereas both modifiers boosted activity, CQDs provided greater enhancements in degradation rates and hydrogen production. These characteristics resulted in remarkable photocatalytic activity, attaining 99 % degradation of MB, 49 % degradation of MO, and 74 % degradation of phenol. Furthermore, the hydrogen production rate attained 1005 μmol/g.h in the QFC sample. In general, both Ag and CQDs improved the efficiency of the Fe2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production; however, CQDs showed superior effectiveness compared to Ag for total efficiency.
K. Kalantarian: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. S. Sheibani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.
